title: Unique Subset

content.problemStatement:
You are given a set of integers represented by an array arrarrarr , which may contain duplicate elements. Your task is to write a recursive function to generate all unique subsets of this array. Each subset should be unique, even if the array contains duplicate elements. The solution should not generate any duplicate subsets.

A subset of a set is a collection of elements that are all derived from the original set. Subsets can range from the empty set (containing no elements) to the set itself (containing all elements).

Function DeclarationFunction NameuniqueSubsetsuniqueSubsetsuniqueSubsets – This function generates all **unique subsets** of a given integer array that may contain duplicate values.

Parameters
multisetmultisetmultiset : A reference to an integer array representing the elements of the set.
Return Value
Returns a **2D array (list of lists)** containing all unique subsets of the given array.
Each subset should appear exactly once, even if the input array contains duplicate elements.
Constraints:
1≤n≤10 1 \leq n \leq 101≤n≤10
1≤arr[i]≤25 1 \leq arr[i] \leq 251≤arr[i]≤25

Input Format

The first line contains one integer nnn, the size of the array.                                                                                                               - Next line contains nnn integers arr[0],arr[1]...arr[n]arr[0],arr[1]...arr[n]arr[0],arr[1]...arr[n], representing the elements of the array.


Output Format

Complete the uniqueSubsets function to generate all the subsets.
Return a 2D array of unique Subsets.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 10), the size of the array. Second line contains N space‑separated integers arr[i] (1 ≤ arr[i] ≤ 25).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing a Python‑style list representation of all unique subsets of the given multiset. Subsets are ordered as produced by a backtracking algorithm that first sorts the array and then explores elements from left to right, skipping duplicates. Example: [[], [1], [1, 2], [2]]

sampleTestCases.explanation: The array [1,2] has four unique subsets. After sorting, backtracking generates them in the order: empty set, [1], [1,2], [2].
Sorting gives [1,2,2]. The algorithm skips the second 2 at the same recursion level, producing six distinct subsets.
All elements are identical; unique subsets correspond to choosing 0,1,2 or 3 copies of 2.

solution.approach: Sort the input array so that equal elements become adjacent. Then use a classic backtracking (depth‑first search) that builds subsets element by element. At each recursion level we iterate from the current start index to the end of the array. To avoid generating duplicate subsets we skip an element if it is equal to the previous one and the previous one was not chosen in the current recursive call (i.e., i>start && arr[i]==arr[i‑1]). Every time we reach a recursion node we add a copy of the current path to the answer list. After the search finishes we print the collected subsets in the required list format. The algorithm runs in O(2^N) in the worst case (when all elements are distinct) and O(N log N) for sorting, which easily satisfies the constraints (N ≤ 10).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N·2^N) per test case (dominated by the number of generated subsets) plus O(N log N) for sorting.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N·2^N) to store all subsets plus O(N) recursion stack.
